title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: vignette covers advanced usage patterns bHIVE: Hyperparameter tuning swarmbHIVE() across tasks Multilayer hierarchical refinement honeycombHIVE() Gradient-based prototype refinement refineB() Integration caret framework Visualization visualizeHIVE()

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: swarmbHIVE() performs grid search bHIVE hyperparameters evaluates combination using task-specific metric.

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
code: data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4])  grid <- expand.grid(   nAntibodies = c(10, 20, 30),   beta        = c(3, 5),   epsilon     = c(0.01, 0.05) )  set.seed(42) tuning <- swarmbHIVE(X = X, task = "clustering", grid = grid,                      metric = "silhouette", maxIter = 15, verbose = FALSE)  ggplot(tuning$results, aes(x = nAntibodies, y = metric_value,                             color = factor(beta))) +   geom_line() +   geom_point(aes(shape = factor(epsilon)), size = 3) +   labs(title = "Clustering: Silhouette Score",        x = "Number of Antibodies", y = "Silhouette",        color = "Beta", shape = "Epsilon") +   scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE) +   theme_minimal() tuning$best_params ##   nAntibodies beta epsilon metric_value ## 4          10    5    0.01    0.2945964

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
code: y <- iris$Species grid_cls <- expand.grid(   nAntibodies = c(15, 30, 50),   beta        = c(3, 5, 10),   epsilon     = c(0.01, 0.05) )  set.seed(42) tuning_cls <- swarmbHIVE(X = X, y = y, task = "classification",                          grid = grid_cls, metric = "accuracy",                          maxIter = 15, verbose = FALSE) tuning_cls$best_params ##    nAntibodies beta epsilon metric_value ## 12          50    3    0.05    0.9533333

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: swarmbHIVE() accepts BPPARAM argument parallel execution. especially useful grid large:
code: library(BiocParallel)  # Use 4 cores tuning <- swarmbHIVE(   X = X, y = y, task = "classification",   grid = grid_cls, metric = "accuracy",   BPPARAM = MulticoreParam(4),   verbose = FALSE )

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: honeycombHIVE() runs bHIVE iteratively across multiple layers. layer produces prototypes become input next layer, creating hierarchical compression data.

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
code: set.seed(42) res <- honeycombHIVE(X = X, task = "clustering",                      layers = 3, nAntibodies = 30,                      beta = 5, epsilon = 0.05,                      maxIter = 10, verbose = FALSE)  # Each layer progressively refines cluster structure layer_info <- sapply(res, function(r) {   c(n_prototypes = nrow(r$antibodies),     n_clusters = length(unique(r$membership))) }) layer_info ##              [,1] [,2] [,3] ## n_prototypes   30   19   13 ## n_clusters     22   14    9

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: collapseMethod parameter controls clusters summarized prototypes next layer:
code: set.seed(42) res_centroid <- honeycombHIVE(X = X, task = "clustering", layers = 2,                               nAntibodies = 20, collapseMethod = "centroid",                               verbose = FALSE) res_medoid <- honeycombHIVE(X = X, task = "clustering", layers = 2,                             nAntibodies = 20, collapseMethod = "medoid",                             verbose = FALSE)  cat("Centroid clusters:", length(unique(res_centroid[[2]]$membership)), "\n") ## Centroid clusters: 10 cat("Medoid clusters:  ", length(unique(res_medoid[[2]]$membership)), "\n") ## Medoid clusters:   13

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: Setting refine = TRUE applies gradient-based updates via refineB() layer’s bHIVE pass. fine-tunes prototype positions collapsing.
code: data(iris) X_iris <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y_iris <- iris$Species  set.seed(42) res_plain <- honeycombHIVE(X = X_iris, y = y_iris, task = "classification",                            layers = 3, nAntibodies = 30, verbose = FALSE)  res_refined <- honeycombHIVE(X = X_iris, y = y_iris, task = "classification",                              layers = 3, nAntibodies = 30,                              refine = TRUE, refineOptimizer = "adam",                              refineLoss = "categorical_crossentropy",                              refineSteps = 5, refineLR = 0.01, verbose = FALSE)  acc_plain   <- mean(res_plain[[3]]$predictions   == as.character(y_iris)) acc_refined <- mean(res_refined[[3]]$predictions == as.character(y_iris)) cat("Accuracy (plain):  ", round(acc_plain, 3), "\n") ## Accuracy (plain):   0.333 cat("Accuracy (refined):", round(acc_refined, 3), "\n") ## Accuracy (refined): 0.333

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: refineB() takes trained antibody positions fine-tunes using gradient-based optimization. supports 5 optimizers 8 loss functions.

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
code: X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y <- iris$Species  set.seed(42) res <- bHIVE(X, y, task = "classification", nAntibodies = 10,              beta = 5, epsilon = 0.05, initMethod = "random",              k = 4, verbose = FALSE)  Ab <- res$antibodies colnames(Ab) <- colnames(X) assignments <- as.integer(factor(res$assignments,                                  levels = unique(res$assignments)))  pca <- prcomp(X, scale. = TRUE) X_pca <- pca$x[, 1:2] A_orig_pca <- predict(pca, Ab)  optimizers <- c("sgd", "adam", "rmsprop") refined_list <- lapply(optimizers, function(opt) {   Ab_refined <- refineB(A = Ab, X = X, y = y,                         assignments = assignments,                         task = "classification",                         loss = "categorical_crossentropy",                         optimizer = opt, steps = 5, lr = 0.01,                         verbose = FALSE)   colnames(Ab_refined) <- colnames(X)   A_ref_pca <- predict(pca, Ab_refined)   data.frame(optimizer = opt,              PC1_after = A_ref_pca[, 1],              PC2_after = A_ref_pca[, 2]) })  refined_df <- do.call(rbind, refined_list) refined_df$ID <- factor(rep(seq_len(nrow(Ab)), times = length(optimizers)))  proto_df <- data.frame(ID = factor(seq_len(nrow(A_orig_pca))),                        PC1_before = A_orig_pca[, 1],                        PC2_before = A_orig_pca[, 2])  merged <- merge(proto_df, refined_df, by = "ID")  ggplot() +   geom_point(data = data.frame(PC1 = X_pca[, 1], PC2 = X_pca[, 2],                                 Cluster = factor(assignments)),              aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Cluster), alpha = 0.4) +   geom_point(data = proto_df,              aes(x = PC1_before, y = PC2_before),              color = "#440154", size = 4) +   geom_point(data = merged,              aes(x = PC1_after, y = PC2_after),              color = "#FDE725", size = 4) +   geom_segment(data = merged,                aes(x = PC1_before, y = PC2_before,                    xend = PC1_after, yend = PC2_after),                arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm"), type = "closed"),                color = "black", linewidth = 0.5) +   scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE) +   labs(title = "Prototype Movement by Optimizer",        subtitle = "Purple = before, Yellow = after refinement",        x = "PC1", y = "PC2") +   facet_wrap(~optimizer, nrow = 1,              labeller = as_labeller(c(sgd = "SGD", adam = "Adam",                                       rmsprop = "RMSProp"))) +   theme_minimal()

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: bHIVE provides two caret-compatible model objects use train(): bHIVEmodel – single-pass bHIVE tuning nAntibodies, beta, epsilon honeycombHIVEmodel – multilayer bHIVE additional tuning layers, refineOptimizer, refineSteps, refineLR

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
code: library(caret)  data(iris) X_iris <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y_iris <- iris$Species  set.seed(42) idx <- sample(nrow(X_iris), nrow(X_iris) * 0.7) X_train <- X_iris[idx, ] X_test  <- X_iris[-idx, ] y_train <- y_iris[idx] y_test  <- y_iris[-idx]  ctrl <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3)  model <- train(   x = X_train, y = y_train,   method = bHIVEmodel,   trControl = ctrl,   tuneGrid = expand.grid(     nAntibodies = c(10, 20),     beta        = c(3, 5),     epsilon     = c(0.01, 0.05)   ),   verbose = FALSE )  ggplot(model) +   labs(title = "caret Cross-Validation Results") +   scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE) +   theme_minimal()

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
code: preds <- predict(model, newdata = X_test) table(Predicted = preds, Actual = y_test) ##             Actual ## Predicted    setosa versicolor virginica ##   setosa         12          2         0 ##   versicolor      0         13        18 ##   virginica       0          0         0

title: Advanced Tuning & Workflows
text: multilayer caret model adds layer count refinement parameters tuning grid:
code: model_hc <- train(   x = X_train, y = y_train,   method = honeycombHIVEmodel,   trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3),   tuneGrid = expand.grid(     nAntibodies = c(20, 30),     beta        = 5,     epsilon     = 0.05,     layers      = c(2, 3),     refineOptimizer = "adam",     refineSteps     = 5,     refineLR        = 0.01   ),   verbose = FALSE )

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: bHIVE package implements Artificial Immune Network (AI-Net) algorithm clustering classification tasks. Inspired biological immune systems, bHIVE uses principles like clonal selection, mutation, suppression analyze model data. vignette demonstrates : 1. Perform clustering classification using bHIVE. 2. Tune hyperparameters using swarmbHIVE. 3. Use caret wrapper easy integration machine learning workflows. 4. Use multilayered immune networks honeycombHIVE 5. Visualize results ggplot2.

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: behavior bHIVE function can fine-tuned using range hyperparameters. description key parameters:

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: Affinity Calculation: Measures similarity data point antibodies using specified affinityFunc. Clonal Expansion Mutation: Generates clones top-matching antibodies, introducing diversity via mutation. Network Suppression: Removes antibodies similar maintain diversity population. Assignment: Assigns data points antibodies (clusters, labels, numeric predictions).

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: following example demonstrates configure bHIVE function clustering, emphasizing impact key parameters:
code: # Load the Iris dataset data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4])  # Configure bHIVE parameters for clustering set.seed(42) res <- bHIVE(   X = X,                      # Input data   task = "clustering",        # Task type   nAntibodies = 20,           # Number of antibodies   beta = 5,                   # Clone multiplier   epsilon = 0.01,             # Suppression threshold   maxIter = 20,               # Maximum iterations   affinityFunc = "gaussian",  # Affinity function   distFunc = "euclidean",     # Distance function   verbose = TRUE              # Print progress )

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: Clustering unsupervised learning task group similar data points based features. example, cluster numeric features Iris dataset using bHIVE.
code: # Load Iris dataset data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4])  # Perform clustering set.seed(42) res <- bHIVE(X = X,               task = "clustering",               nAntibodies = 10,               beta = 5,               epsilon = 0.05,               maxIter = 20,               k = 3,              verbose = FALSE)  # Add cluster assignments to the data iris$Cluster <- as.factor(res$assignments)  # Visualize clusters ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length,                   y = Sepal.Width,                   color = Cluster)) +     geom_point(size = 3) +     labs(title = "Clustering Results with bHIVE",           x = "Sepal Length",           y = "Sepal Width") +     scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE) +      theme_minimal()

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: Classification supervised learning task data points assigned predefined categories based features. , classify species Iris flowers using bHIVE.  Comparing predicted vs actual
code: # Classification setup y <- iris$Species  # Perform classification set.seed(42) res <- bHIVE(X = X,               y = y,               task = "classification",               nAntibodies = 100,               beta = 5,               epsilon = 0.05,               initMethod = "random",              k = 4,              verbose = FALSE)  # Visualize classification results iris$Predicted <- res$assignments ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length,                   y = Sepal.Width,                   color = Predicted,                   shape = Species)) +     geom_point(size = 3) +     labs(title = "Classification Results with bHIVE",           x = "Sepal Length",           y = "Sepal Width") +     scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE) +      theme_minimal() table(Predicted = res$assignments, Actual = y) ##             Actual ## Predicted    setosa versicolor virginica ##   setosa         50          0         0 ##   versicolor      0         37         1 ##   virginica       0         13        49

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: Tuning hyperparameters crucial optimizing performance machine learning algorithms. example, perform hyperparameter tuning clustering using swarmbHIVE.  Best parameters
code: grid <- expand.grid(     nAntibodies = c(10, 20),     beta = c(3, 5),     epsilon = c(0.01, 0.05) )  # Perform tuning set.seed(42) tuning_results <- swarmbHIVE(X = X,                               task = "clustering",                               grid = grid,                               metric = "silhouette",                              verbose = FALSE)  # Visualize tuning results ggplot(tuning_results$results, aes(x = nAntibodies,                                     y = metric_value,                                     color = factor(beta))) +     geom_line() +     geom_point(aes(shape = as.factor(epsilon)),                     size = 3) +     labs(title = "Hyperparameter Tuning Results",           x = "Number of Antibodies",           y = "Silhouette Score",           color = "Beta",           shape = "Epsilon") +     scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE) +      theme_minimal() tuning_results$best_params ##   nAntibodies beta epsilon metric_value ## 1          10    3    0.01    0.1875808

text: bHIVE package provides caret wrapper seamless integration caret framework, allowing easy cross-validation hyperparameter tuning. , demonstrate classification iris dataset.
code: data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y <- iris$Species  # Splitting Training and Validation Data Sets set.seed(42) sample.idx <- sample(nrow(X), nrow(X)*0.7) x_test <- X[sample.idx,] x_val <- X[-sample.idx,] y_test <- y[sample.idx] y_val <- y[-sample.idx]   train_control <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 2) set.seed(42) model <- train(x = x_test,                y = y_test,                method = bHIVEmodel,                trControl = train_control,                tuneGrid = expand.grid(                  nAntibodies = c(10, 20, 30),                  beta = c(3, 5, 10),                  epsilon = c(0.01, 0.05, 0.1)),                verbose = FALSE)  # Visualize caret results ggplot(model) +     labs(title = "Caret Tuning Results for bHIVE",          x = "Hyperparameter Combination",          y = "Performance Metric") +     scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE) +     theme_minimal()

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: honeycombHIVE, clustering proceeds hierarchically across multiple layers: honeycombHIVE iteratively builds refines network “antibodies” (prototypes) capture complex patterns data. Initially, bHIVE algorithm creates set prototypes, layer can fine-tuned using gradient-based updates (via refineB() function) flexible optimizers like SGD, Adam, RMSProp. Fo layer, collapse step aggregates refined prototypes - computing centroid, medoid, another statistic - compressing data new, lower-dimensional representation prototype becomes new observation. new representation serves input next layer, similar neural network architecture.  Note: use task = "classification", honeycombHIVE generate multi-layer predictions. can compare layer’s predictions true labels see performance improves data become collapsed.
code: # Load the Iris dataset data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4])  # Run honeycombHIVE for clustering res <- honeycombHIVE(X = X,                       task = "clustering",                       epsilon = 0.05,                      layers = 3,                       nAntibodies = 30,                       beta = 5,                       maxIter = 10,                       verbose = FALSE)  # Visualize results from each layer for (i in seq_along(res)) {      # Create a data frame for plotting; add original Sepal.Length and Sepal.Width   plot_df <- data.frame(     Sepal.Length = iris$Sepal.Length,      Sepal.Width  = iris$Sepal.Width,     Cluster      = factor(res[[i]]$membership)  # cluster labels from layer i   )      # Basic ggplot scatter plot   plot <- ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = Sepal.Length,                                y = Sepal.Width,                                color = Cluster)) +     geom_point(size = 3) +     labs(       title = paste("honeycombHIVE Clustering - Layer", i),       x     = "Sepal Length",       y     = "Sepal Width"     ) +     theme_minimal() +     scale_color_viridis(discrete = TRUE)      print(plot) }

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: refineB() function takes prototypes produced bHIVE() algorithm fine-tunes using gradient‐based updates. addition basic parameters: loss: loss function used guide refinement. classification task, “categorical_crossentropy” typical choice. steps: number gradient update iterations. lr: learning rate update. sgd: Standard stochastic gradient descent. momentum: SGD momentum. (Control momentum momentum_coef). adagrad: Adaptive gradient descent. adam: Adaptive moment estimation. (Control decay rates beta1 beta2). rmsprop: Root Mean Square Propagation. (Control decay squared gradient average rmsprop_decay)  Interpretation * Data points shown light transparency, colored cluster assignments (determined original bHIVE algorithm). * Initial prototypes shown dark purple. prototypes (antibodies) refinement. * Refined prototypes shown bright yellow. arrows indicate direction magnitude update original positions refined positions.
code: # Prepare the Iris dataset X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y <- iris$Species  # Run bHIVE to obtain initial antibody prototypes. set.seed(42) res <- bHIVE(X = X,               y = y,               task = "classification",               nAntibodies = 10,               beta = 5,               epsilon = 0.05,               initMethod = "random",              k = 4,              verbose = FALSE)  Ab <- res$antibodies colnames(Ab) <- colnames(X) assignments <- res$assignments  # Ensure assignments are numeric indices. assignments <- as.integer(factor(assignments, levels = unique(assignments)))  # PCA of the Iris data for visualization in 2D pca <- prcomp(X, scale. = TRUE) X_pca <- pca$x[, 1:2] colnames(X_pca) <- c("PC1", "PC2") # Transform initial prototypes into PCA space. A_bhive_pca <- predict(pca, Ab)  # Run refinement using several optimizers. optimizers <- c("sgd", "adam", "rmsprop") refined_list <- lapply(optimizers, function(opt) {   Ab_refined <- refineB(A = Ab,                         X = X,                          y = y,                         assignments = assignments,                         loss = "categorical_crossentropy",                          task = "classification",                          steps = 5,                          lr = 0.01,                         verbose = FALSE,                         optimizer = opt,                         beta1 = 0.9,                         beta2 = 0.999,                         rmsprop_decay = 0.9)   colnames(Ab_refined) <- colnames(X)   A_refined_pca <- predict(pca, Ab_refined)   data.frame(optimizer = opt,              PC1_after = A_refined_pca[,1],              PC2_after = A_refined_pca[,2]) }) refined_df <- do.call(rbind, refined_list) refined_df$optimizer <- factor(refined_df$optimizer, levels = optimizers)

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
code: data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y <- iris$Species  res_class <- honeycombHIVE(X = X,                            y = y,                            layers = 3,                            task = "classification",                            nAntibodies = 30,                            beta = 5,                            epsilon = 0.01,                            verbose = FALSE)  res_class_refine <- honeycombHIVE(X = X,                                   y = y,                                   task = "classification",                                   layers = 3,                                   nAntibodies = 30,                                   beta = 5,                                   epsilon = 0.01,                                   refine = TRUE,                                   refineOptimizer = "adam",                                   refineLoss = "categorical_crossentropy",                                   refineSteps = 3,                                   refineLR = 0.01,                                   verbose = FALSE)  table(Refined = res_class_refine[[3]]$predictions,       Actual  = y) ##         Actual ## Refined  setosa versicolor virginica ##   setosa     50         50        50

title: The buzz on using bHIVE
text: example run honeycombHIVE() Iris dataset clustering task. scatterplot generated using PCA transformation reduce feature space two dimensions. Data points colored cluster membership (treated discrete) prototypes overlaid black points.  scatterplot shows data projected onto first two principal components. data point colored according cluster (discrete grouping), prototypes computed honeycombHIVE displayed large black points. Faceting layer applied multiple layers selected.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: article describes mathematical formulation biological motivation every component bHIVE. intended reference companion tutorial vignettes organized mirror algorithm’s execution flow: initialization, affinity computation, clonal selection, mutation, regulation, assignment. Throughout, use following notation:

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: immunology, shape space (Perelson & Oster 1979) abstract space antibody’s binding site (paratope) antigen’s epitope represented points. Affinity function distance space, closer shapes bind tightly. shape space framework provides geometric foundation AIS algorithms: data points antigens, prototypes antibodies, learning process moving antibodies shape space maximize affinity data.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: bHIVE computes n×mn \times m affinity matrix 𝐅\mathbf{F} Fij=f(𝐱i,𝐚j)F_{ij} = f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{}_j). Five kernels available:

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: fgauss(𝐱,𝐚)=exp⁡(−α∥𝐱−𝐚∥2)f_{\text{gauss}}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{}) = \exp\!\bigl(-\alpha \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{}\|^2\bigr) default kernel. bandwidth parameter α\alpha controls quickly affinity decays distance. Larger α\alpha produces sharper, localized affinity peaks.

previous_headings: Shape Space and Affinity > Affinity kernels
title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: flap(𝐱,𝐚)=exp⁡(−α∥𝐱−𝐚∥)f_{\text{lap}}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{}) = \exp\!\bigl(-\alpha \|\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{}\|\bigr) Similar Gaussian decays L1-like (linear) distance rather squared distance. Produces heavier tails, making robust outliers.

previous_headings: Shape Space and Affinity > Affinity kernels
title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: fpoly(𝐱,𝐚)=(𝐱⋅𝐚+c)pf_{\text{poly}}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{}) = (\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{} + c)^p dot-product kernel cc bias term pp polynomial degree. Captures non-Euclidean similarity structure. Useful relevant signal angle magnitude feature vectors rather Euclidean distance.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: fcos(𝐱,𝐚)=𝐱⋅𝐚∥𝐱∥∥𝐚∥+ϵf_{\text{cos}}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{}) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{}}{\|\mathbf{x}\| \|\mathbf{}\| + \epsilon} Direction-based similarity, invariant vector magnitude. small ϵ=10−12\epsilon = 10^{-12} prevents division zero. Appropriate feature magnitudes uninformative (e.g., normalized expression profiles).

previous_headings: Shape Space and Affinity > Affinity kernels
title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: fham(𝐱,𝐚)=1−1d∑k=1d𝟏[xk≠ak]f_{\text{ham}}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{}) = 1 - \frac{1}{d}\sum_{k=1}^d \mathbf{1}[x_k \neq a_k] Proportion matching features. Used categorical binary data.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: clustering assignment network suppression, bHIVE uses distance rather affinity. Six metrics available:

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: bHIVE provides four initialization strategies starting antibody population: kmeans++ initialization (Arthur & Vassilvitskii 2007) selects first antibody uniformly random, subsequent antibody probability proportional squared distance nearest existing antibody: P(𝐱i)∝min⁡j<current∥𝐱i−𝐚j∥2P(\mathbf{x}_i) \propto \min_{j < \text{current}} \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{}_j\|^2.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: Real antibody diversity generated V(D)J recombination: variable region immunoglobulin gene assembled randomly combining one V (variable), one D (diversity), one J (joining) gene segment germline library. combinatorial mechanism generates approximately 101110^{11} distinct antibodies $$300 gene segments.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: VDJLibrary translates feature space: Segment feature space three groups (V, D, J) splitting dd dimensions. Three methods available: PCA: first ⌊d/3⌋\lfloor d/3 \rfloor principal components form V, next form D, remainder form J Cluster: k-means clustering within dimension group creates alleles Random partition: Dimensions randomly assigned V, D, J Create alleles segment clustering data projected onto segment’s dimensions (using k-means nV, nD, nJ centers respectively) Generate antibodies combinatorial sampling: select one V allele, one D allele, one J allele, concatenate form complete dd-dimensional antibody vector produces structured coverage data manifold nV×nD×nJn_V \times n_D \times n_J potential combinations, analogous combinatorial diversity real immune system.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: Clonal selection theory (Burnet 1959) states antigen enters body, B cells whose receptors best match antigen selected proliferate (clonal expansion) undergo mutation (somatic hypermutation). result iterative refinement process: population antibodies evolves toward higher affinity antigen. mechanism de Castro & Von Zuben (2001) formalized AI-Net algorithm bHIVE implements C++ acceleration.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: iteration t=1,…,Tt = 1, \ldots, T: Step 1: Affinity computation. Compute n×mn \times m affinity matrix 𝐅\mathbf{F} using chosen kernel. expensive step dispatched BLAS via RcppArmadillo. Step 2: Clonal expansion mutation. data point 𝐱i\mathbf{x}_i: Select kk antibodies highest affinity: 𝒦i=top-kjf(𝐱i,𝐚j)\mathcal{K}_i = \text{top-}k_j\, f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{}_j) selected antibody 𝐚j∈𝒦i\mathbf{}_j \\mathcal{K}_i, generate ncn_c clones : nc=round(β⋅f(𝐱i,𝐚j)),nc≤𝚖𝚊𝚡𝙲𝚕𝚘𝚗𝚎𝚜n_c = \text{round}\!\left(\beta \cdot f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{}_j)\right), \quad n_c \leq \texttt{maxClones} Mutate clone: 𝐚′=𝐚j+𝛜\mathbf{}' = \mathbf{}_j + \boldsymbol{\epsilon} 𝛜\boldsymbol{\epsilon} drawn active SHM strategy (see ). mutant higher affinity parent, replaces parent. Step 3: Network regulation. Suppress redundant antibodies (see Idiotypic Network section) apply simple ϵ\epsilon-threshold suppression: remove 𝐚j\mathbf{}_j d(𝐚i,𝐚j)<ϵd(\mathbf{}_i, \mathbf{}_j) < \epsilon <ji < j. Step 4: Task-specific assignment. Clustering: data point assigned nearest antibody distance Classification: antibody labeled majority vote assigned data points; predictions via affinity-weighted nearest antibody Step 5: Convergence check. Early stopping antibody population size changed stopTolerance noImprovementLimit consecutive iterations.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: Somatic hypermutation process activated B cells introduce point mutations antibody genes rate approximately 10610^6 times higher background mutation rate. enzyme activation-induced cytidine deaminase (AID) preferentially targets WRCY/RGYW DNA motifs (hotspots), creating non-uniform mutation landscape. Mutations improve antigen binding selected ; reduce lead cell death. multiple rounds mutation selection (affinity maturation), antibody population converges high-affinity binders.

text: 𝐚′=𝐚+𝒩(0,(1−f)⋅γt−1)\mathbf{}' = \mathbf{} + \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\; (1 - f) \cdot \gamma^{t-1}\right) ff affinity parent antibody, γ\gamma mutationDecay, tt iteration. mutation rate decreases affinity (better antibodies mutate less) time (population stabilizes). use: Default baseline. Watkins & Timmis (2004), achieves approximately 50% better data reduction uniform mutation concentrating mutation low-affinity antibodies. use: want mutation rate strictly controlled affinity, tunable temperature.

Features larger discrepancies mutate , analogous AID targeting specific DNA motifs. use: features informative others want algorithm discover automatically.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: ∥𝛜∥2≤E0⋅(1−f)2\|\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\|^2 \leq E_0 \cdot (1 - f)^2 total mutation magnitude bounded energy budget shrinks affinity increases. Inspired Kleinstein’s observation energetic cost SHM scales quadratically number mutations (ESHM∼Nmut2E_{\text{SHM}} \sim N_{\text{mut}}^2). Individual feature perturbations sampled uniformly, rescaled satisfy budget constraint. use: want hard bounds much single mutation event can perturb antibody.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: Implementation convergence hypothesis. feature kk antibody: gk=xk−ak(gradient toward matched antigen)g_k = x_k - a_k \quad \text{(gradient toward matched antigen)} mk←β1mk+(1−β1)gk(first moment)m_k \leftarrow \beta_1 \, m_k + (1 - \beta_1)\, g_k \quad \text{(first moment)} vk←β2vk+(1−β2)gk2(second moment)v_k \leftarrow \beta_2 \, v_k + (1 - \beta_2)\, g_k^2 \quad \text{(second moment)} ϵk=base_rate⋅mkvk+ϵadam\epsilon_k = \text{base\_rate} \cdot \frac{m_k}{\sqrt{v_k} + \epsilon_{\text{adam}}} identical Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba 2015) applied per-feature. biological interpretation: mkm_k represents cell’s “memory” direction mutate (accumulated selection pressure), vkv_k tracks variance past selection signals (stability gradient). Features consistent directional pressure get larger mutations; features noisy signals damped. use: Complex landscapes different features different scales signal--noise ratios. Best general-purpose choice.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: Jerne’s idiotypic network theory (1974) proposes antibodies don’t just recognize antigens, also recognize . variable region one antibody (idiotype) can serve epitope another antibody (anti-idiotype). creates regulatory network emergent properties: memory, tolerance, self-organized repertoire structure. Varela & Coutinho (1991) formalized “second generation” immune network bell-shaped activation function: lower threshold θlow\theta_{\text{low}}, insufficient stimulation leads cell death (neglect); thresholds, moderate stimulation leads activation; θhigh\theta_{\text{high}}, excessive stimulation leads suppression. biologically realistic simple ϵ\epsilon-threshold suppression used classical AIS, produces qualitatively different repertoire dynamics.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: Given antibody matrix 𝐀\mathbf{} mm rows: Step 1: Compute m×mm \times m pairwise affinity matrix 𝐒\mathbf{S} Sij=f(𝐚i,𝐚j)S_{ij} = f(\mathbf{}_i, \mathbf{}_j). Step 2: pair (,j)(, j), compute activation signal: hij={−1if Sij<θlow(death signal)+1if θlow≤Sij≤θhigh(activation)−1if Sij>θhigh(suppression)h_{ij} = \begin{cases} -1 & \text{} S_{ij} < \theta_{\text{low}} \quad \text{(death signal)} \\ +1 & \text{} \theta_{\text{low}} \leq S_{ij} \leq \theta_{\text{high}} \quad \text{(activation)} \\ -1 & \text{} S_{ij} > \theta_{\text{high}} \quad \text{(suppression)} \end{cases} Step 3: Euler-integrate population dynamics ODE TT steps step size Δt\Delta t: dAidt=s−δ⋅Ai+Ai∑j:hij>0Sij−Ai∑j:hij<0Sij\frac{dA_i}{dt} = s - \delta \cdot A_i + A_i \sum_{j: h_{ij} > 0} S_{ij} - A_i \sum_{j: h_{ij} < 0} S_{ij} : - ss = source_rate (basal production new cells) - δ\delta = decay_rate (natural death) - activation suppression terms weighted actual affinity values Step 4: Remove antibodies whose population level AiA_i falls survival_threshold.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: germinal center (GC) specialized microstructure within lymph nodes B cells undergo iterative rounds mutation (dark zone) selection (light zone). light zone, B cells compete help T follicular helper (Tfh) cells. Tfh cell evaluates quality B cell’s antigen presentation provides survival signals best competitors. B cells fail receive Tfh help undergo apoptosis. quality-control bottleneck: ensures improved antibodies survive round.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: GerminalCenter module implements resource competition: Compute quality score qjq_j antibody 𝐚j\mathbf{}_j. scoring task-aware: Classification: qjq_j = proportion assigned data points whose label matches antibody’s majority label Clustering: qjq_j = mean affinity assigned data points (cohesion) Apply selection pressure p∈[0,1]p \[0, 1] compute survival threshold: τ=qmin+p⋅(qmax−qmin)\tau = q_{\min} + p \cdot (q_{\max} - q_{\min}) nTfh helper cells, Tfh selects antibody highest quality among yet helped. Antibodies threshold τ\tau receive Tfh help removed. Repeat rounds cycles.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: B cell fate strongly influenced tissue microenvironment: chemokine gradients direct migration, cytokines influence differentiation, local cell density affects competition resources. germinal center, dark zone (proliferation/mutation) light zone (selection) create spatially distinct functional regions.

text: Microenvironment module classifies antibody’s local context computing kernel density estimates data: ρj=1n∑=1nf(𝐱i,𝐚j)\rho_j = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{=1}^n f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{}_j) Based percentile ρj\rho_j within antibody population: PhighP_{\text{high}} PlowP_{\text{low}} high_density_threshold low_density_threshold percentiles, respectively.

text: module also computes directional gradient antibody, pointing toward local center mass nearby data: 𝐠j=∑(𝐱i,𝐚j)⋅𝐱i∑(𝐱i,𝐚j)−𝐚j\mathbf{g}_j = \frac{\sum_i f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{}_j) \cdot \mathbf{x}_i}{\sum_i f(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{}_j)} - \mathbf{}_j gradient can bias mutation direction toward higher-density regions, analogous chemokine-directed B cell migration.

title: Algorithm & Biological Foundations
text: real immunity, B cell activation requires two signals: Signal 1: Specific antigen recognition B cell receptor (affinity-dependent) Signal 2: Costimulatory signals helper T cells, danger signals (DAMPs/PAMPs), cytokines two-signal model (Bretscher & Cohn 1970, formalized AIS Freitas 2006) prevents autoimmune activation: B cell binds self-antigen without costimulation anergized (silenced) rather activated. serves biologically-principled regularization mechanism.

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: bHIVE package provides two equivalent interfaces: functional API (bHIVE()) quick, one-liner analysis R6 class API (AINet) full compositional control. vignette focuses R6 API 9 immune modules can injected AINet customize every stage algorithm. module independent R6 class encapsulates single immunological mechanism. create modules separately pass AINet$new(), allowing mix match combination.
code: model <- AINet$new(   nAntibodies = 20,   shm            = SHMEngine$new(method = "adaptive"),   idiotypic      = IdiotypicNetwork$new(),   germinalCenter = GerminalCenter$new(),   microenvironment = Microenvironment$new(),   init           = VDJLibrary$new(method = "pca"),   activation     = ActivationGate$new(),   memory         = MemoryPool$new() )

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: SHMEngine controls antibodies mutate cloning. Five strategies available, grounded different aspect SHM biology: adaptive method distinctive somatic hypermutation Adam optimizer function . feature dimension maintains running mean variance past gradients.
code: data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y <- iris$Species  methods <- c("uniform", "airs", "adaptive") results <- lapply(methods, function(m) {   shm <- SHMEngine$new(method = m)   model <- AINet$new(nAntibodies = 15,                       maxIter = 15,                       shm = shm,                      verbose = FALSE)   model$fit(X, y, task = "classification")   data.frame(     method   = m,     accuracy = mean(model$result$assignments == as.character(y)),     n_ab     = nrow(model$repertoire$as_matrix())   ) }) do.call(rbind, results) ##     method  accuracy n_ab ## 1  uniform 0.9600000   14 ## 2     airs 0.9266667   15 ## 3 adaptive 0.9533333   15

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: classical AIS, similar antibodies suppressed via simple distance threshold (epsilon). IdiotypicNetwork module replaces principled network dynamics inspired Varela & Coutinho’s (1991) second-generation immune network theory. key insight bell-shaped activation function: little stimulation antibodies leads death, moderate stimulation leads activation, excessive stimulation leads suppression. creates emergent self-organized repertoire structure. idiotypic network typically produces parsimonious repertoire regulation based network antibody-antibody interactions rather pairwise distance alone.
code: # Standard epsilon suppression (default) model_std <- AINet$new(nAntibodies = 25, maxIter = 15, verbose = FALSE) model_std$fit(X, task = "clustering")  # Idiotypic network regulation model_idi <- AINet$new(   nAntibodies = 25, maxIter = 15,   idiotypic = IdiotypicNetwork$new(     theta_low = 0.01,      theta_high = 0.5,     source_rate = 0.5,      timeSteps = 20   ),   verbose = FALSE ) model_idi$fit(X, task = "clustering")  cat("Standard suppression:", model_std$repertoire$size(), "antibodies\n") ## Standard suppression: 24 antibodies cat("Idiotypic regulation:", model_idi$repertoire$size(), "antibodies\n") ## Idiotypic regulation: 2 antibodies

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: GerminalCenter module models competition T follicular helper (Tfh) cell help occurs real germinal centers. antibodies receive Tfh help survive – implements quality-based selection pressure. quality metric task-aware: Classification: proportion correctly matched labels Clustering: local density (sum affinities assigned data points)
code: gc <- GerminalCenter$new(   nTfh = 10,                 # number of Tfh selectors   selectionPressure = 0.5,   # 0 = no selection, 1 = very strict   rounds = 1 )  model_gc <- AINet$new(   nAntibodies = 30,    maxIter = 15,   germinalCenter = gc,   verbose = FALSE ) model_gc$fit(X, y, task = "classification") cat("Antibodies after GC selection:", model_gc$repertoire$size(), "\n") ## Antibodies after GC selection: 10 cat("Accuracy:", mean(model_gc$result$assignments == as.character(y)), "\n") ## Accuracy: 0.9333333

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: Microenvironment module classifies local context around antibody three zones adapts mutation rates accordingly: Stable (high local density): reduce mutation rate preserve good antibodies Explore (low local density): increase mutation rate search sparse regions Boundary (intermediate): moderate mutation, potential trigger class switching
code: me <- Microenvironment$new(   high_density_threshold = 0.75,   low_density_threshold = 0.25,   stabilization_factor = 0.3,   # reduce mutation to 30% in stable zones   exploration_factor = 2.0      # double mutation in exploration zones )  rep <- ImmuneRepertoire$new(X[sample(150, 15), ]) env <- me$assess(rep, X) table(env$zones) ##  ## boundary  explore   stable  ##        7        4        4

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: Instead random sampling, VDJLibrary generates antibodies combinatorial assembly gene segments, mimicking V(D)J recombination. feature space split three segments (V, D, J), discretized alleles, new antibodies built combining one allele segment. Three decomposition methods available: "pca": PCA components define gene segments (best correlated features) "cluster": k-means within dimension groups creates alleles "random_partition": random feature grouping (simplest)
code: vdj <- VDJLibrary$new(nV = 5,                        nD = 3,                        nJ = 3,                        method = "pca") A <- vdj$generate(20, X) dim(A)  # 20 antibodies x 4 features ## [1] 20  4 print(vdj) ## <VDJLibrary> method='pca' V=5 D=3 J=3 ##   Combinatorial space: 45 antibodies

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: real immunity, B cells require two signals activate: antigen recognition (Signal 1) costimulatory context (Signal 2). ActivationGate module prevents spurious cloning isolated outliers. Signal 2 options: "density": local data density around antibody "danger": user-provided danger score per data point "entropy": local label entropy (classification )
code: gate <- ActivationGate$new(   signal2_type = "density",   threshold1 = 0.1,    # minimum affinity (Signal 1)   threshold2 = 0.3     # density percentile (Signal 2) )  A <- X[sample(150, 10), ] rep <- ImmuneRepertoire$new(A) aff <- rep$affinity_matrix(X, "gaussian") activated <- gate$evaluate(aff, X, A) cat("Activated interactions:", sum(activated), "/", length(activated), "\n") ## Activated interactions: 501 / 1500

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: MemoryPool archives high-performing antibodies recalls relevant current data. useful streaming non-stationary data distribution shifts may occur.
code:
mp <- MemoryPool$new(   archive_threshold = 0.01,  # minimum average affinity to archive   max_memory = 50,   recall_threshold = 0.01 )  rep <- ImmuneRepertoire$new(X[sample(150, 10), ]) n_archived <- mp$archive(rep, X) cat("Archived:", n_archived, "memory cells
") ## Archived: 10 memory cells cat("Pool size:", mp$size(), "
") ## Pool size: 10 # Recall memories relevant to a subset recalled <- mp$recall(X[1:30, ]) cat("Recalled:", nrow(recalled), "cells for the query
") ## Recalled: 3 cells for the query

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: ClassSwitcher module changes effective kernel width antibodies based microenvironment zone, analogous real B cell isotype switching:
code: cs <- ClassSwitcher$new(alpha_IgM = 0.1, alpha_IgG = 5.0, alpha_IgA = 1.0)  rep <- ImmuneRepertoire$new(X[sample(150, 10), ]) zones <- sample(c("stable", "explore", "boundary"), 10, replace = TRUE) alphas <- cs$switch_isotypes(rep, zones) data.frame(zone = zones, isotype = rep$metadata$isotype, alpha = alphas) ##        zone isotype alpha ## 1  boundary     IgA   1.0 ## 2    stable     IgG   5.0 ## 3    stable     IgG   5.0 ## 4    stable     IgG   5.0 ## 5  boundary     IgA   1.0 ## 6    stable     IgG   5.0 ## 7   explore     IgM   0.1 ## 8    stable     IgG   5.0 ## 9    stable     IgG   5.0 ## 10  explore     IgM   0.1

title: Composing Immune Modules
text: ConvergentSelector identifies “public antibodies” – antibodies appear across multiple independent bHIVE runs. implements immunological concept public clonotypes biologically-motivated ensemble method.
code: # Run 3 independent bHIVE analyses set.seed(42) results <- lapply(1:3, function(i) {   m <- AINet$new(nAntibodies = 15, maxIter = 10, verbose = FALSE)   m$fit(X, task = "clustering")   m$result })  conv <- ConvergentSelector$new(tolerance = 1.0, min_appearances = 2) public <- conv$from_results(results) cat("Public antibodies:", nrow(public), "\n") ## Public antibodies: 14

title: bHIVE - B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine
text: bHIVE R package implementing modular Artificial Immune System (AIS) framework clustering classification. Built AI-Net (de Castro & Von Zuben 2001), bHIVE extends classical algorithm biologically-grounded modules drawn modern immunology: somatic hypermutation, idiotypic network regulation, germinal center selection, microenvironment-driven adaptation. Performance-critical operations (affinity/distance matrices, clonal selection, network suppression, mutation) implemented C++ via RcppArmadillo, parallelization support BiocParallel.

title: bHIVE - B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine
text: Two tasks – clustering classification numeric matrices C++ backend – BLAS-optimized bulk affinity/distance computation Two APIs – functional (bHIVE()) quick use R6 (AINet$new()) full module composition Multilayer architecture – honeycombHIVE() hierarchical prototype refinement across layers Hyperparameter tuning – swarmbHIVE() grid search BiocParallel Gradient refinement – refineB() post-processing 5 optimizers several classification-aware loss functions Composable immune modules – mix match biological mechanisms via dependency injection caret compatible – bHIVEmodel honeycombHIVEmodel cross-validation workflows

title: bHIVE - B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine
text: full control, compose AINet combination immune modules. module optional slots fit loop appropriate biological stage: richer composition uses microenvironment-aware exploration, Tfh-mediated quality selection, density-driven isotype switching, persistent memory pool:
code: # Adaptive mutation + idiotypic network regulation model <- AINet$new(   nAntibodies = 20,   maxIter = 30,   shm = SHMEngine$new(method = "adaptive", base_rate = 0.1),   idiotypic = IdiotypicNetwork$new(theta_low = 0.01, theta_high = 0.5),   verbose = FALSE ) model$fit(X, iris$Species, task = "classification") table(model$result$assignments)  # Predict on new data preds <- model$predict(X[1:10, ]) me  <- Microenvironment$new()                              # zone classification cs  <- ClassSwitcher$new(alpha_IgM = 0.1, alpha_IgG = 5)   # zone -> kernel width gc  <- GerminalCenter$new(nTfh = 8, selectionPressure = 0.6) mp  <- MemoryPool$new(archive_threshold = 0.05)            # persists across fits  model <- AINet$new(   nAntibodies = 30, maxIter = 20,   shm              = SHMEngine$new(method = "hotspot"),   microenvironment = me,   classSwitcher    = cs,            # requires microenvironment   germinalCenter   = gc,   memory           = mp,   verbose          = FALSE ) model$fit(X, iris$Species, task = "classification")  # Memory persists -- a second fit will recall relevant cells (clustering only) # and continue archiving high-affinity antibodies. mp$size()

title: bHIVE - B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine
text: bHIVE evolves population antibody vectors represent structure data. iteration runs subset following stages — modules attach specific stages skipped absent: Initialization () – sample data, random generation, kmeans++, V(D)J combinatorial assembly via VDJLibrary. MemoryPool carries cells prior fit, relevant memory recalled merged starting repertoire (clustering ). Activation gating – ActivationGate sets aside antibodies -dense neighborhoods affinity floor clonal selection runs sparse subset; gated antibodies rejoin . Clonal selection + SHM – top-k antibodies cloned per data point. Mutation dispatches SHMEngine (uniform, airs, hotspot, energy, adaptive); adaptive strategy threads per-antibody Adam-style moment matrices across iterations. Germinal center selection – GerminalCenter runs Tfh-mediated quality selection; survivors weighted clustering compactness classification purity. Microenvironment & class switching – Microenvironment classifies antibody stable / explore / boundary zones applies density-dependent jitter. ClassSwitcher binds zone isotype (IgM/IgG/IgA) sets next iteration’s kernel width. Idiotypic regulation – IdiotypicNetwork runs bell-shaped Ab-Ab dynamics cull -clumped isolated antibodies. Falls back top-population safety net ill-tuned thresholds kill repertoire. Network suppression – removes near-duplicate antibodies within epsilon-ball chosen distance metric. Orphan pruning + final assignment () – antibodies bind training point dropped; remaining cells produce cluster IDs class predictions. MemoryPool archives high-affinity survivors back pool.

title: bHIVE - B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine
code: # honeycombHIVE: hierarchical prototype refinement res <- honeycombHIVE(X, y = iris$Species, task = "classification",                      layers = 3, nAntibodies = 30,                      refine = TRUE, refineOptimizer = "adam")  # swarmbHIVE: hyperparameter grid search (parallelizable) grid <- expand.grid(nAntibodies = c(15, 30), beta = c(3, 5), epsilon = c(0.01, 0.1)) best <- swarmbHIVE(X, y = iris$Species, task = "classification",                    grid = grid, metric = "accuracy", maxIter = 20) best$best_params

title: bHIVE - B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine
text: Fine-tune antibody positions training refineB():
code: res <- bHIVE(X, y = iris$Species, task = "classification",              nAntibodies = 20, maxIter = 20)  # Adam-based refinement with cross-entropy loss A_refined <- refineB(res$antibodies, X, y = iris$Species,                      assignments = res$assignments,                      task = "classification",                      loss = "categorical_crossentropy",                      optimizer = "adam", steps = 10, lr = 0.01)

title: ClassSwitcher — ClassSwitcher

title: SHMEngine — SHMEngine
text: Somatic Hypermutation Engine implementing five biologically- inspired mutation strategies bHIVE artificial immune system.

title: SHMEngine — SHMEngine
text: five strategies : uniform Classic random Gaussian noise. Mutation rate = (1-affinity) *     decay^(iter-1). original bHIVE behavior. airs AIRS-style affinity-proportional mutation. Rate = c * exp(-affinity / T).     Watkins & Timmis (AIRS2), achieving 50% better data reduction uniform. hotspot Feature-importance-weighted mutation. Features higher gradient     magnitude mutate , analogous AID targeting WRCY motifs real SHM. energy Energy-budget-constrained mutation. Total mutation magnitude bounded     E = E_0 * (1-affinity)^2, inspired Kleinstein's E_SHM ~ N_Mut^2 model. adaptive Per-feature adaptive mutation rate moment tracking, directly     implementing SHM Adam optimizer.

title: SHMEngine — SHMEngine
text: method Character. One "uniform", "airs", "hotspot", "energy", "adaptive". params Named list method-specific parameters. m1_state First moment state matrix (adaptive method). m2_state Second moment state matrix (adaptive method).

Inspired biological immune systems, employs clonal selection, mutation, network suppression analyze model datasets. package provides flexible functionality, including affinity metrics, mutation strategies, hyperparameter tuning.

text: Maintainer: Nick Borcherding ncborch@gmail.com

what: bHIVE: B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine — bHIVE
title: bHIVE: B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine — bHIVE
text: Implements artificial immune network algorithm clustering classification tasks. algorithm evolves population "antibodies" via clonal selection mutation, applies network suppression maintain diversity, assigns data points based affinity distance metrics.

title: bHIVE: B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine — bHIVE
code: bHIVE(   X,   y = NULL,   task = NULL,   nAntibodies = 20,   beta = 5,   epsilon = 0.01,   maxIter = 50,   affinityFunc = "gaussian",   distFunc = "euclidean",   affinityParams = list(alpha = 1, c = 1, p = 2, Sigma = NULL),   mutationDecay = 1,   mutationMin = 0.01,   maxClones = Inf,   stopTolerance = 0,   noImprovementLimit = Inf,   initMethod = c("sample", "random", "random_uniform", "kmeans++"),   k = 3,   verbose = TRUE )

title: bHIVE: B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine — bHIVE
text: X numeric matrix data frame input features, rows observations columns features. y Optional. factor target vector classification. NULL, clustering performed. task Character. Specifies task perform: "clustering" "classification". NULL, inferred based y. nAntibodies Integer. initial population size antibodies. beta Numeric. Clone multiplier (controls many clones generated top-matching antibodies). epsilon Numeric. Similarity threshold used network suppression; antibodies closer epsilon considered redundant. maxIter Integer. Maximum number iterations run AI-Net algorithm. affinityFunc Character. Specifies affinity (similarity) function use antibody-data matching. One "gaussian", "laplace", "polynomial", "cosine", "hamming". distFunc Character. Specifies distance function clustering suppression. One "euclidean", "manhattan", "minkowski", "cosine", "mahalanobis", "hamming". affinityParams list optional parameters chosen affinity distance function. alpha (RBF Laplace kernel), c, p (polynomial kernel Minkowski distance), Sigma (Mahalanobis distance). mutationDecay Numeric. Factor mutation rate decays iteration (\(\le 1.0\)). Default 1.0 (decay). mutationMin Numeric. Minimum mutation rate, preventing mutation scale shrinking zero. maxClones Numeric. Maximum number clones per top-matching antibody; defaults Inf. stopTolerance Numeric. change number antibodies (repertoire size) \(\le stopTolerance\) consecutive iterations, may trigger noImprovementLimit. noImprovementLimit Integer. Stops algorithm early improvement antibody count (beyond stopTolerance) many consecutive iterations. Default Inf, meaning early stop based improvement. initMethod Character. Method initializing antibodies. Can : "sample" - randomly selects rows X initial   antibodies. "random" - samples Gaussian noise using X's column   means/sds. "random_uniform" - samples uniformly [min, max]   column. "kmeans++" - tries kmeans++-like initialization   coverage. k Integer. Number top-matching antibodies (affinity) consider cloning data point. verbose Logical. TRUE, prints progress messages iteration.

title: bHIVE: B-cell Hybrid Immune Variant Engine — bHIVE
text: list: antibodies: Final antibody vectors (nAntibodies x nFeatures). assignments:         - clustering: integer cluster IDs [1..#Antibodies].         - classification: predicted labels. task: chosen task.

what: honeycombHIVE: Multilayer AIS with optional gradient-based fine-tuning — honeycombHIVE
title: honeycombHIVE: Multilayer AIS with optional gradient-based fine-tuning — honeycombHIVE
text: honeycombHIVE function implements multilayer artificial immune system iteratively refines set prototypes - referred antibodies - model structure input data. layer, function first uses bHIVE algorithm generate update antibodies based current data representation task (clustering classification). Optionally, applies gradient-based fine-tuning (via refineB) antibodies, allowing advanced refinement various optimizers (e.g., SGD, Adam, RMSProp) customizable loss functions. final output hierarchical set layers encapsulate refined prototypes corresponding cluster assignments predictions original observations, making honeycombHIVE versatile tool adaptive learning pattern recognition.

text: Numeric matrix (nAb x d) antibody/prototype positions. X Matrix data frame (nSamples x d) feature data. y Optional. Factor (classification) NULL (clustering). assignments Integer character vector (length = nSamples), specifying antibody index (label) sample belongs . task One c("clustering", "classification"). loss One c("categorical_crossentropy", "binary_crossentropy", "kullback_leibler", "cosine", "mae"). steps Integer. many gradient steps run. lr Numeric. Learning rate update. push_away Logical (classification). Whether push prototypes away differently-labeled samples. verbose Logical. Momentum coefficient (used optimizer=="momentum"). beta1 Numeric. Exponential decay rate first moment estimates (used Adam). beta2 Numeric. title: refineB: Gradient-based fine-tuning for bHIVE antibodies with multiple loss functions and optimizers — refineB
text: Updated matrix shape (nAb x d).

title: refineB: Gradient-based fine-tuning for bHIVE antibodies with multiple loss functions and optimizers — refineB
text: user must provide: - numeric matrix antibody/prototype positions (nAb x nFeatures). - numeric matrix/data frame X data (nSamples x nFeatures). - Optional y classification. task="clustering",   y can NULL ignored. - integer character vector assignments (length=nSamples) giving   antibody index (label) data point assigned. ## Available Losses ### Classification (factor y) - **"categorical_crossentropy"**: Pull prototypes toward data points share   antibody's dominant label; push away otherwise. - **"binary_crossentropy"**: Similar categorical CE, interpret factor y   binary (two classes). Pull label, push different label. - **"kullback_leibler"**: rough approach treats "dominant label vs. others"   p q distributions, pushing/pulling prototypes. - **"cosine"**: Interpreted trying maximize cosine similarity -label points   minimize different-label points. - **"mae"**: Sign-based pull/push. ## Available Optimizers - **"sgd"**: Basic stochastic gradient descent. - **"momentum"**: SGD momentum. - **"adagrad"**: Adaptive gradient descent. - **"adam"**: Adaptive moment estimation. - **"rmsprop"**: Root Mean Square Propagation. function performs gradient-based updates antibody using selected loss function. Depending chosen optimizer, may use plain SGD, momentum-based updates, Adagrad, Adam, RMSProp.

title: refineB: Gradient-based fine-tuning for bHIVE antibodies with multiple loss functions and optimizers — refineB
code: data(iris) X <- as.matrix(iris[, 1:4]) y <- iris$Species res <- bHIVE(X, y, task = "classification", nAntibodies = 10,              maxIter = 5, verbose = FALSE) assignments <- as.integer(factor(res$assignments,                                  levels = unique(res$assignments))) A_refined <- refineB(res$antibodies, X, y = y,                      assignments = assignments,                      task = "classification",                      loss = "categorical_crossentropy", optimizer = "adam",                      steps = 3, lr = 0.01, verbose = FALSE) dim(A_refined) #> [1] 10  4

Perform hyperparameter tuning for classification tuning_results <- swarmbHIVE(X = X,                               y = y,                               task = "classification",                               grid = grid,                               metric = "balanced_accuracy",                              maxIter = 10) #> Starting swarmbHIVE with 8 parameter combinations (task=classification, metric=balanced_accuracy). #> Evaluating combo 1/8: nAntibodies=10, beta=3, epsilon=0.010 #> Evaluating combo 2/8: nAntibodies=20, beta=3, epsilon=0.010 #> Evaluating combo 3/8: nAntibodies=10, beta=5, epsilon=0.010 #> Evaluating combo 4/8: nAntibodies=20, beta=5, epsilon=0.010 #> Evaluating combo 5/8: nAntibodies=10, beta=3, epsilon=0.050 #> Evaluating combo 6/8: nAntibodies=20, beta=3, epsilon=0.050 #> Evaluating combo 7/8: nAntibodies=10, beta=5, epsilon=0.050 #> Evaluating combo 8/8: nAntibodies=20, beta=5, epsilon=0.050 #> Best parameters found: #>   nAntibodies beta epsilon metric_value #> 8          20    5    0.05    0.7933333  # title: bHIVE 0.99.3
text: Modules previously accepted AINet$new() stored algorithm never invoked fit loop. v0.99.3 wires every module AINet$fit() affects predictions: init (VDJLibrary object exposing $generate(n, X)) now controls initial-repertoire construction. supplied, overrides initMethod. makes V(D)J combinatorial assembly actual starting state rather dead metadata. idiotypic (IdiotypicNetwork) now invoked clonal selection network suppression. bell-curve idiotypic_dynamics_cpp kernel culls antibodies -clumped niches (suppression) isolated antibodies (insufficient stimulation). safety net keeps top-10% population ill-tuned thresholds kill every antibody, iteration can continue downstream selection can penalize config. activation (ActivationGate) now pre-screens antibodies -dense feature-space neighborhoods round clonal selection. Gated antibodies sit aside unchanged selection runs sparse subset, rejoin repertoire. classification mode class labels refreshed majority-vote nearest data points pre-allocated random labels poison final predictions. microenvironment (Microenvironment) assessed iteration clonal selection. mutation_modifiers scale per-antibody Gaussian jitter applied feature units (0.5% feature SD iter 1, decaying mutationDecay). Sparse-region antibodies explore outward, dense-region antibodies stabilize. germinalCenter (GerminalCenter) now runs Tfh-mediated quality selection clonal-selection rejoin. Survivor indices composed across selection rounds mirrored onto antibody_classes adaptive-SHM moment matrices external per-antibody state stays aligned. GerminalCenter$select() now returns composed survivor vector (relative input repertoire) exposes $last_survivors. classSwitcher (ClassSwitcher) runs microenvironment binds antibody’s isotype (IgM/IgG/IgA) zone. population-mean per-isotype alpha used next iteration’s affinity kernel width, modulating matching breadth based current density landscape. Requires microenvironment present. memory (MemoryPool) invoked two points. Pre-iteration, clustering mode, relevant memory cells prior fits recalled (via recall(X)) merged starting repertoire prior knowledge seeds search. Post-iteration, orphan pruning, surviving high-affinity antibodies archived back pool (archive()) persist across fits. Classification archives also attach class_label repertoire metadata future consumers can read back; recall suppressed classification avoid label loss. shm (SHMEngine) now dispatched inside clonal_selection_iteration_cpp rather ignored. five strategies — uniform, airs, hotspot, energy, adaptive — produce distinct mutation behavior. adaptive strategy’s Adam-style first/second moment matrices threaded C++ kernel re-aligned R every subset/reorder operation (activation gating, germinal-center selection, idiotypic culling, network suppression) per-antibody moment tracking stays consistent across iterations.

title: bHIVE 0.99.3
text: clonal_selection_iteration_cpp now accepts SHM dispatch parameters (shm_method, shm_c_rate, shm_temperature, shm_E_0, shm_base_rate, shm_beta1, shm_beta2, shm_adam_epsilon) plus adaptive moment matrices m1_state/m2_state. R wrapper provides defaults (shm_method = "uniform", empty state matrices) legacy callers pass original 15 arguments still get previous behavior. New src/shm.h exposes five SHM strategy helpers mutate_by_method dispatcher shared shm_mutate_cpp clonal_selection_iteration_cpp. helpers longer static.

title: bHIVE 0.99.3
text: Orphan-antibody pruning: AINet$fit() now drops surviving antibodies nearest neighbor training point computing final assignment. Eliminates ghost antibodies pass suppression bind nothing, tightens relationship cluster count effective antibody count. antibody_classes now pre-allocated iteration loop classification mode (uniform random levels(y)), new pre-selection gate logic value index iteration 1 rather referencing -yet-defined variable.

title: bHIVE 0.99.2
text: Removed regression task support across package. previous regression implementation unreliable redesigned. bHIVE(), honeycombHIVE(), swarmbHIVE(), refineB(), AINet, GerminalCenter, bHIVEmodel, honeycombHIVEmodel, visualizeHIVE() longer accept task = "regression" numeric y. Removed regression-loss functions (mse, huber, poisson) regression metrics (rmse, mae, r2) swarmbHIVE(). Removed refineHuberDelta parameter honeycombHIVE() honeycombHIVEmodel. final_assignment_cpp() longer accepts antibody_values overall_mean arguments.

title: bHIVE 0.99.1
text: Added RcppArmadillo backend BLAS-optimized bulk affinity distance matrix computation, replacing per-element R loops Scalar affinity function clone/mutate hot path avoids 1x1 matrix allocation overhead C++ implementations clonal selection iteration, network suppression, kmeans++ initialization, final assignment, somatic hypermutation (5 methods), idiotypic network dynamics

title: bHIVE 0.99.1
text: New ImmuneRepertoire class antibody collections metadata tracking (isotype, state, age, lineage) New ImmuneAlgorithm abstract base class fit/predict/summary interface New AINet class wrapping core algorithm composable module injection R6 composition functional API equally supported

title: bHIVE 0.99.1
text: SHMEngine — Five somatic hypermutation strategies: uniform (original behavior), airs (affinity-proportional), hotspot (feature-gradient-weighted), energy (budget-constrained), adaptive (per-feature Adam-like moment tracking) IdiotypicNetwork — Antibody-antibody network dynamics bell-shaped activation function replacing epsilon-threshold suppression GerminalCenter — T-follicular helper mediated selection task-aware quality scoring resource competition Microenvironment — Density-dependent zone classification (stable/explore/boundary) chemokine-like gradient computation VDJLibrary — Combinatorial V(D)J gene library initialization via PCA, k-means clustering, random partition feature space ActivationGate — Two-signal activation gate requiring antigen recognition costimulatory context (density, danger, entropy) MemoryPool — Archive high-affinity antibodies long-lived memory cells threshold-based recall ClassSwitcher — Isotype class switching (IgM broad, IgG specific, IgA boundary) modulating effective kernel width ConvergentSelector — Cross-repertoire consensus identification public antibodies ensemble methods

title: bHIVE 0.99.1
